perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and interpreted in accordance with the laws of the Province of Ontario.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and interpreted in accordance with the laws of intellectual property applicable internationally.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law clause to 'intellectual property applicable internationally' is too vague and could create confusion and make it difficult to determine which specific jurisdiction's laws apply. This introduces ambiguity and potential unenforceability. The location for the changed text remains the same.
perturbation.contradicted_law: The Hague Convention on Choice of Court Agreements
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 3 requires the court chosen to have exclusive jurisdiction over the agreement. By stating 'intellectual property applicable internationally,' the choice of court is made ambiguous and non-exclusive, possibly leading to challenges on the court's jurisdiction over the agreement.
perturbation.location: Last paragraph before the 'In Witness Whereof' section
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Article III | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Article III Article III refers to Article III of the Constitution , the section of the Constitution that authorizes a federal court system . This provides that federal courts have judicial power over all cases and controversies arising under the Constitution, laws, or treaties of the United States; of admiralty and maritime jurisdiction ; in which the United States is a party; between two or more states; between a state and the citizens of another state; between citizens of different states; between citizens of the same state claiming lands under grants of different states; and between a state or citizens thereof and foreign states, citizens, or subjects. An Article III judge is a judge who is authorized under Article III of the Constitution. An Article III court is a court that Congress establishes pursuant to Article III of the Constitution. [Last reviewed in March of 2025 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Keywords constitutional law Wex admiralty/maritime CIVICS the Constitution COMMERCE commercial activities transportation THE LEGAL PROCESS courts judicial administration legal practice/ethics wex definitions constitutional law courts and procedure government legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies a clear governing law (Province of Ontario). The changed text introduces ambiguity by referring to 'intellectual property applicable internationally,' which doesn't define a specific jurisdiction. This contradicts the need for a clear choice of law, as highlighted in the Law Explanation about exclusive jurisdiction, creating a structural flaw. While Scraped Law Snippet 2 mentions Article III of the Constitution, its relevance is limited as it broadly defines federal court powers, not specific requirements for choice of law clauses. The Perturbation Explanation correctly identifies that the vagueness of the changed text creates potential unenforceability. The change introduces ambiguity and potential confusion, making it difficult to determine which specific jurisdiction's laws apply, undermining the contract's enforceability and creating a significant legal contradiction, because it is a Structural Flaw that creates a Legal Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: For a period of five (5) years from the date of receiving it, all Confidential Information will be maintained in confidence by the receiving party, will not be disclosed to any third party or to any persons empl oyed in its business other than those having a need to know for the purposes set fo rth above, and will be protected with the same degree of care as the recei ving party normally uses in the protection of its own confidential and proprietary information, but in no case with any less degree than reasonable care.
perturbation.changed_text: For a period of five (5) years from the date of receiving it, all Confidential Information will be maintained in confidence by the receiving party, will not be disclosed to any third party or to any persons empl oyed in its business other than those having a need to know for the purposes set fo rth above. However, confidential information may be disclosed if it is deemed beneficial by the receiving party.
perturbation.explanation: Adding 'However, confidential information may be disclosed if it is deemed beneficial by the receiving party' introduces a significant loophole that undermines the confidentiality obligation. This creates ambiguity and weakens the enforceability of the confidentiality clause.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Ontario's Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act (PIPEDA)
perturbation.law_explanation: Clause 4.5 of PIPEDA requires organizations to protect personal information. Introducing a broad 'deemed beneficial' exception allows the receiving party to disclose confidential information without adhering to the standards required by PIPEDA, creating a contradiction.

perturbation.justification: The original text ensures confidentiality with reasonable care. The changed text introduces an exception allowing disclosure if 'deemed beneficial,' directly contradicting the intent of maintaining confidentiality. The Law Explanation mentions PIPEDA requiring organizations to protect personal information, and the added clause undermines these safeguards by allowing arbitrary disclosure. The snippet provided names PIPEDA and its principles which include accountability, limiting use, disclosure and retention, and safeguards.

perturbation.original_text: If either party elects not to pursue any further business und ertaking, each party will promptly return upon request all tangible informati on including any and all copies thereof relating to all Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: If either party elects not to pursue any further business und ertaking, each party will promptly return all tangible informati on including any and all copies thereof relating to all Confidential Information unless deemed necessary for future reference.
perturbation.explanation: Adding 'unless deemed necessary for future reference' creates a significant ambiguity and weakens the obligation to return confidential information. This change makes it more difficult to enforce the return of confidential materials and contradicts standard confidentiality practices.
perturbation.law_citation: Common Law Principles of Trade Secret Protection
perturbation.law_explanation: While Canada does not have specific trade secret legislation, trade secrets are protected under common law principles of confidentiality and contractual obligations. By adding the 'unless deemed necessary' clause, the modified text allows the receiving party to retain confidential information without a clear legitimate purpose, which could weaken the trade secret protection afforded to the disclosing party.

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces an exception ('unless deemed necessary for future reference') to the obligation to return confidential information. This weakens the original requirement and introduces ambiguity regarding what constitutes 'necessary,' potentially allowing a party to retain confidential information indefinitely, undermining the protection of trade secrets. While the snippets are limited, the explanation highlights that this weakens trade secret protection under common law principles, aligning with the 'Structural Flaws Legal Contradiction' definition.

perturbation.original_text: The furnishing of Confidential Information pursu ant to this Agreement shall not be construed as granting or conferring, either expr essly or implicitly, any rights, licences or relationships.
perturbation.changed_text: The furnishing of Confidential Information pursu ant to this Agreement shall not be construed as granting or conferring, either expr essly or implicitly, any rights, licences or relationships, unless otherwise agreed upon verbally.
perturbation.explanation: Adding 'unless otherwise agreed upon verbally' opens the door to potential disputes and disagreements about the terms of the agreement. It creates a possibility that rights, licenses, or relationships could be established verbally, contradicting the written agreement's disclaimer of such implications.
perturbation.law_citation: Ontario Statute of Frauds, R.S.O. 1990, c. S.19
perturbation.law_explanation: The Ontario Statute of Frauds requires certain contracts to be in writing to be enforceable. Adding a clause that allows for verbal agreements to modify the terms of the confidentiality agreement could render it unenforceable in certain situations, as it contradicts the requirement for written agreements.

S.19, Statute of Frauds, R.S.O. 1990, c. S.19 Act Statute of Frauds R.S.O. 1990, Chapter S.19 Consolidation Period: Â From December 9, 1994 to the e-Laws currency date . Last amendment: 1994, c. 27, s. 55. Legislative History: 1994. c. 27, s. 55. Writing required to create certain estates or interests 1 (1) Every estate or interest of freehold and every uncertain interest of, in, to or out of any messuages, lands, tenements or hereditaments shall be made or created by a writing signed by the parties making or creating the same, or their agents thereunto lawfully authorized in writing, and, if not so made or created, has the force and effect of an estate at will only, and shall not be deemed or taken to have any other or greater force or effect. Leases to be made by deed (2) All leases and terms of years of any messuages, lands, tenements or hereditaments are void unless made by deed. R.S.O. 1990, c.Â S.19, s.Â 1. How leases or estates of freehold, etc., to be granted or surrendered 2 Subject to section 9 of the Conveyancing and Law of Property Act , no lease, estate or interest, either of freehold or term of years, or any uncertain interest of, in, to or out of any messuages, lands, tenements or hereditaments shall be assigned, granted or surrendered unless it be by deed or note in writing signed by the party so assigning, granting, or surrendering the same, or the partyâs agent thereunto lawfully authorized by writing or by act or operation of law. R.S.O. 1990, c.Â S.19, s.Â 2. Except leases not exceeding three years, etc. 3 Sections 1 and 2 do not apply to a lease, or an agreement for a lease, not exceeding the term of three years from the making thereof, the rent upon which, reserved to the landlord during such term, amounts to at least two-thirds of the full improved value of the thing demised. perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: n 7 provides that all conveyances in trusts of land must be in writing signed by the maker or by will. Section 9 provides that all grants and assignments of trusts in land must be in writing signed by the grantor or by will. Section 8 excepts from section 7 and 9 trusts that arise or result by implication of construction of law i.e. resulting trusts and constructive trusts . Sections 1 to 3 and 7 to 9 and 24 were repealed by section 207 of, and Schedule 7 to, the Law of Property Act 1925 ( 15 & 16 Geo. 5 . c. 20). However section 53(1)(a) required that interest in land be created or disposed by a signed writing, a will or operation of law. Section 53(1)(b) requires a declaration of trust in land must be by a signed writing or a will and section 53(1)(c) requires the same disposition of equitable interests and existing trusts. Section 52(2) state that section 53 does not affect the creation or operation of implied, resulting or constructive trusts. Section 54 provides interests in land created by parol and not put in writing and signed have the force and effect of interests at will only except that the lease for 3 years or less at the best rent may be made by parol. Section 4 [ edit ] Section 4 of the Statute of Frauds [ 5 ] originally provided that an action may not be brought on the following types of contracts unless there is a written note or memorandum signed by the party being charged or a person authorized by them: Contracts by the executor of a will to pay a debt of the estate with his own money. Contracts in which one party becomes a surety (acts as guarantor) for another party's debt or other obligation. Contracts in consideration of marriage . Contracts for the transfer of an interest in land . Contracts that cannot be performed within one year . This section now provides that contracts of guarantee (surety for another's debt) are unenforceable unless evidenced in writing. This requirement is subject to section 3 of the Mercantile Law Amendment Act 1856 ( 19 & 20 Vict. c.. 97) [ 6 ] which provides that the consideration for the guarantee need not appear in writing or by necessary inference from a written document. Section 6 of the Statute of Frauds Amendment Act 1828 ( 9 Geo. 4 . c. 14) [ 7 ] (commonly known as Lord Tenterden's Act [ 8 ] ) was enacted to prevent clause 2 section 4 being circumvented by bringing an action for the tort of deceit (the tort in Freeman v. Palsey [ 9 ] ). In this section, the words "or upon any contract or sale of lands, tenements or hereditaments or any interest in or concerning them" were repealed by section 207 of, and Schedule 7 to, the Law of Property Act 1925 ( 15 & 16 Geo. 5 . c. 20). However the requirement that contract for sale of land must be in writing was continued by section 40 of that act. [ 10 ] Section 40 of the Law of Property Act 1925 was repealed by sections 2(8) and 4 of, and Schedule 2 to, the Law of Property (Miscellaneous Provisions) Act 1989 ; however, section 2 of that act requires that contracts for the sale of land be signed and in writing. United Kingdom legislation Law Reform (Enforcement of Contracts) Act 1954 Act of Parliament Parliament of the United Kingdom Long title An Act to amend section four of the Statute of Frauds, 1677; and to repeal section four of the Sale of Goods Act, 1893. Citation 2 & 3 Eliz. 2 . c. 34 Dates Royal assent 4 June 1954 Other legislation Amends Statute of Frauds Sale of Goods Act 1893 Text of statute as originally enacted All the clauses except the one relating to surety contracts were repealed by section 1 of the Law Reform (Enforcement of Contracts) Act 1954 ( 2 & 3 Eliz. 2 . c. 34). [ 11 ] This section does not apply (if it would otherwise do so) in relation to a financial collateral arrangement. [ 12 ] In 1937, the Law Revision Committee recommended that this section be repealed. perturbation.justification: The modified text adds 'unless otherwise agreed upon verbally,' which introduces a significant contradiction to the Statute of Frauds. The Law Explanation states that the Statute of Frauds requires certain contracts to be in writing to be enforceable. Scraped Law Snippet 1 supports this, stating that 'Every estate or interest of freehold...shall be made or created by a writing signed by the parties'. By allowing verbal agreements to alter the contract, the modified text potentially undermines the requirement for written agreements, thus creating a contradiction and potentially rendering the agreement unenforceable under the Statute of Frauds. Additionally, Scraped Law Snippet 2 says 'contracts for the transfer of an interest in land...must be in writing' further highlighting how verbal agreements could create a legal contradiction.
